SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper makes clear, testable claims and presents experiments (toy problems and MNIST) showing brute-force pruning outperforms first- and second-order approximations; however important methodological details and verifications are missing. Key derivations are deferred to an appendix (“a complete step-by-step derivation ... is provided in the Supplementary Material”), so the correctness of the second-order formulae and Hessian approximations cannot be checked from the main text. Experimental methodology has weaknesses: use of a small 5k MNIST subset and compressed inputs, no statistical significance testing, and a narrow set of baselines (only first-order and brute-force) limit the generality of the claims. The authors also rely on unsupported assumptions (diagonal-only Hessian, serial greedy pruning) which they acknowledge are flawed, creating logical gaps between theory and the complex behaviors they observe.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies existing ideas (Taylor approximations, Optimal Brain Damage/Skeletonization-style reasoning and the Mozer & Smolensky observation) to the neuron-level pruning problem and proposes a second-order Taylor criterion for whole-neuron removal, which is a modest, sensible extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Pruning entire neurons (rather than individual weights) and comparing brute-force, first-order and second-order criteria is a useful perspective, but the core techniques and assumptions are clearly rooted in prior work (LeCun et al., Hassibi & Stork, Mozer & Smolensky) as the authors themselves note. The framing and extensive empirical analysis provide some new insights about representation sparsity, but the methodological novelty is incremental.

CLARITY.justification: Overall organization follows a standard structure (introduction, related work, methods, experiments, conclusions) and algorithms are explicitly described (Algorithm 1 and 2), but presentation issues reduce clarity. Several problems appear in the text: repeated paragraphs, missing figure references (e.g. “Figure ??”), LaTeX/artifact remnants, and frequent deferral of key math to the appendix which makes the main exposition harder to follow. Figures are frequently referenced and discussed in detail but are not present here, and some claims (e.g. precise pruning percentages) are stated without accompanying statistical summaries, which hampers reproducibility and reader comprehension.
